## Abstract

Algae plays a key role in carbon capture and utilization (CCU) as it can capture and use the atmospheric CO2 for conversion of value-added products. Concentrated CO2 is common in flue gas and provides opportunities for algae cultivation. The drawbacks are mass transfer limitation, poor CO2 dissolution, and challenges to reach optimal levels for algal growth at given flue gas levels. Bicarbonate is flexible to be used as carbon source and owns the potential to enhance the efficiency of biological carbon fixation by algae. The requirements of algae strains are more stringent. To improve the industrial scale-up of CCU, system optimization is of great importance. More novel algal strains that can grow rapidly under harsh environment and provide valuable bio-products should be developed for large-scale production. Algae-driven CCU is promising for achieving carbon-neutrality.

**Keywords:**
Algae; Bicarbonate; Carbon capture and utilization; Carbon-neutrality; Concentrated CO(2).

--- SOURCE 3: A Review of Algae-Based Carbon Capture, Utilization, and Storage ... ---
URL: https://www.mdpi.com/2673-5628/4/4/24

SUMMARY:
Journals Journals Find a Journal Journal Journals Algae-based CCUS is integral to the BECCS framework, leveraging algae’s biological processes to capture and sequester CO2 while simultaneously contributing to energy production and potentially achieving net negative carbon emissions. This comprehensive review explores recent innovations in algae-based CCUS technologies, focusing on the mechanisms of carbon capture, utilization, and storage through algae. It highlights advancements in algae cultivation for efficient carbon capture, algae-based biofuel production, and algae-based dual carbon storage materials, as well as key challenges that need to be addressed for further optimization. In response to this challenge, carbon capture, utilization, and storage (CCUS) technologies have emerged as a key solution to address excessive CO2 emissions from fossil fuel use [17,18].

Abstract
--------

Excessive emissions of greenhouse gases, primarily carbon dioxide (CO2), have garnered worldwide attention due to their significant environmental impacts. Carbon capture, utilization, and storage (CCUS) techniques have emerged as effective solutions to address CO2 emissions. Recently, direct air capture (DAC) and bioenergy with carbon capture and storage (BECCS) have been advanced within the CCUS framework as negative emission technologies. BECCS, which involves cultivating biomass for energy production, then capturing and storing the resultant CO2 emissions, offers cost advantages over DAC. Algae-based CCUS is integral to the BECCS framework, leveraging algae’s biological processes to capture and sequester CO2 while simultaneously contributing to energy production and potentially achieving net negative carbon emissions. Algae’s high photosynthetic efficiency, rapid growth rates, and ability to grow in non-arable environments provide significant advantages over other BECCS methods. This comprehensive review explores recent innovations in algae-based CCUS technologies, focusing on the mechanisms of carbon capture, utilization, and storage through algae. It highlights advancements in algae cultivation for efficient carbon capture, algae-based biofuel production, and algae-based dual carbon storage materials, as well as key challenges that need to be addressed for further optimization. This review provides valuable insights into the potential of algae-based CCUS as a key component of global carbon reduction strategies.

1\. Introduction
----------------

Carbon dioxide (CO2), one of the primary greenhouse gases, has been steadily increasing in the atmosphere, leading to significant adverse effects globally \[[1](https://www.mdpi.com/2673-5628/4/4/24#B1-gases-04-00024),[2](https://www.mdpi.com/2673-5628/4/4/24#B2-gases-04-00024)\]. These effects include intensified global warming and frequent extreme weather events, which severely threaten human livelihoods, crop production, wildlife habitats, and the overall stability and functionality of ecosystems. Reducing and controlling CO2 emissions to address the challenges posed by climate change has become a critical issue of international concern in recent years. In response, international climate policies such as the United Nations Framework Convention on Climate Change (UNFCCC) \[[3](https://www.mdpi.com/2673-5628/4/4/24#B3-gases-04-00024)\] and the Paris Agreement \[[4](https://www.mdpi.com/2673-5628/4/4/24#B4-gases-04-00024),[5](https://www.mdpi.com/2673-5628/4/4/24#B5-gases-04-00024)\] have driven global efforts to mitigate excessive CO2 emissions and tackle climate change.

To meet CO2 reduction targets, replacing traditional fossil fuels with clean energy sources, such as hydrogen, nuclear power, wind energy, and solar power, is considered an effective strategy \[[6](https://www.mdpi.com/2673-5628/4/4/24#B6-gases-04-00024),[7](https://www.mdpi.com/2673-5628/4/4/24#B7-gases-04-00024),[8](https://www.mdpi.com/2673-5628/4/4/24#B8-gases-04-00024),[9](https://www.mdpi.com/2673-5628/4/4/24#B9-gases-04-00024),[10](https://www.mdpi.com/2673-5628/4/4/24#B10-gases-04-00024),[11](https://www.mdpi.com/2673-5628/4/4/24#B11-gases-04-00024)\]. Fossil fuels produce CO2 during extraction, refining, and transportation, and emit large amounts of CO2 during combustion \[[12](https://www.mdpi.com/2673-5628/4/4/24#B12-gases-04-00024),[13](https://www.mdpi.com/2673-5628/4/4/24#B13-gases-04-00024),[14](https://www.mdpi.com/2673-5628/4/4/24#B14-gases-04-00024),[15](https://www.mdpi.com/2673-5628/4/4/24#B15-gases-04-00024)\]. Given the extensive reliance on fossil fuels for electricity generation, transportation, and industrial processes, this results in substantial CO2 emissions. In contrast, clean energy sources can eliminate these emissions \[[16](https://www.mdpi.com/2673-5628/4/4/24#B16-gases-04-00024)\]. However, the high costs and limited technological infrastructure of clean energy technologies currently restrict their widespread adoption. Thus, fossil fuels remain the dominant global energy source.

In response to this challenge, carbon capture, utilization, and storage (CCUS) technologies have emerged as a key solution to address excessive CO2 emissions from fossil fuel use \[[17](https://www.mdpi.com/2673-5628/4/4/24#B17-gases-04-00024),[18](https://www.mdpi.com/2673-5628/4/4/24#B18-gases-04-00024)\]. CCUS involves capturing CO2 from industrial processes or energy production, utilizing it to create valuable products or chemicals, or securely storing it in underground reservoirs or stable geological formations to prevent its release into the atmosphere. Globally, countries are actively advancing CCUS technologies with significant progress. China, the largest emitter of CO2, has implemented supportive policies for CCUS technologies and projects since the start of its 13th Five-Year Plan in 2016, which has substantially accelerated their development \[[17](https://www.mdpi.com/2673-5628/4/4/24#B17-gases-04-00024)\]. Notable advancements in China include CO2 capture, CO2\-enhanced oil recovery (EOR), CO2\-enhanced gas recovery (EGR), CO2\-enhanced coal bed methane (ECBM), and CO2 saline aquifer storage \[[19](https://www.mdpi.com/2673-5628/4/4/24#B19-gases-04-00024),[20](https://www.mdpi.com/2673-5628/4/4/24#B20-gases-04-00024),[21](https://www.mdpi.com/2673-5628/4/4/24#B21-gases-04-00024),[22](https://www.mdpi.com/2673-5628/4/4/24#B22-gases-04-00024)\]. The United States is also at the forefront of CCUS research and application, bolstered by strong governmental support. Policies such as the 45Q Tax Credit \[[23](https://www.mdpi.com/2673-5628/4/4/24#B23-gases-04-00024),[24](https://www.mdpi.com/2673-5628/4/4/24#B24-gases-04-00024)\], the United States Energy Act of 2020 \[[25](https://www.mdpi.com/2673-5628/4/4/24#B25-gases-04-00024)\], the Infrastructure Investment and Jobs Act \[[26](https://www.mdpi.com/2673-5628/4/4/24#B26-gases-04-00024)\], and the Utilizing Significant Emissions with Innovative Technologies (USE IT) Act \[[27](https://www.mdpi.com/2673-5628/4/4/24#B27-gases-04-00024)\] reflect the U.S. government’s commitment to addressing climate change and achieving emission reduction goals. These policies and economic incentives underscore the vital role of the U.S. in mitigating climate change and advancing global emission reduction efforts. India, following China and the United States, is the third-largest emitter of CO2 globally, due to its rapid industrialization and growing energy demands \[[28](https://www.mdpi.com/2673-5628/4/4/24#B28-gases-04-00024)\]. To address these emissions, India has set ambitious goals in its Intended Nationally Determined Contribution (INDC), aiming to achieve 175,000 MW of renewable energy capacity by 2022 and to ensure that around 40% of its installed capacity comes from non-fossil fuel sources by 2030 \[[29](https://www.mdpi.com/2673-5628/4/4/24#B29-gases-04-00024)\]. While India’s primary focus has been on transitioning to renewable energy and enhancing energy efficiency, there is growing interest in CCUS technologies. Recently, the government launched the National Mission on Carbon Sequestration and Enhanced Oil Recovery (EOR), which targets capturing CO2 from industrial sources for enhanced oil recovery in mature oil fields. This initiative highlights India’s strategy to incorporate CCUS technologies into its climate change mitigation efforts \[[30](https://www.mdpi.com/2673-5628/4/4/24#B30-gases-04-00024)\]. Moreover, India has partnered with several international organizations and is collaborating with other countries to develop and demonstrate CCUS technologies in the Indian context \[[31](https://www.mdpi.com/2673-5628/4/4/24#B31-gases-04-00024),[32](https://www.mdpi.com/2673-5628/4/4/24#B32-gases-04-00024),[33](https://www.mdpi.com/2673-5628/4/4/24#B33-gases-04-00024)\].

Although CCUS technologies have made significant progress in reducing CO2 emissions, achieving net-zero emissions remains a challenge that requires continued technological advancements. Direct air carbon capture and storage (DACCS) and bioenergy with carbon capture and storage (BECCS) are both considered essential for achieving carbon neutrality due to their potential to generate negative emissions \[[34](https://www.mdpi.com/2673-5628/4/4/24#B34-gases-04-00024)\]. DACCS captures and stores low-concentration CO2 directly from the atmosphere, a process that requires highly efficient adsorbents and large-scale infrastructure, resulting in relatively high costs \[[35](https://www.mdpi.com/2673-5628/4/4/24#B35-gases-04-00024),[36](https://www.mdpi.com/2673-5628/4/4/24#B36-gases-04-00024)\]. In contrast, BECCS is more cost-effective, as it produces bioenergy from biomass, and the CO2 released during bioenergy production and utilization is reabsorbed by growing biomass, forming a closed-loop system. Moreover, BECCS generates bioenergy, adding economic value while reducing carbon emissions. Due to its economic and sustainability advantages, BECCS is more favorable for widespread global adoption compared to DACCS.

Algae capture CO2 through photosynthesis, which can be utilized in the commercial production of high-value products such as food, nutraceuticals, pharmaceuticals, and cosmetics. This type of algae-based carbon utilization is well-established globally, with notable implementation in regions such as the southwestern United States, Asia, and Australia \[[37](https://www.mdpi.com/2673-5628/4/4/24#B37-gases-04-00024)\]. Although these applications do not involve bioenergy production, they remain a key part of algae-based carbon utilization. Within the BECCS framework, algae offer distinct advantages over conventional feedstocks like crops, wood, and agricultural residues \[[38](https://www.mdpi.com/2673-5628/4/4/24#B38-gases-04-00024),[39](https://www.mdpi.com/2673-5628/4/4/24#B39-gases-04-00024),[40](https://www.mdpi.com/2673-5628/4/4/24#B40-gases-04-00024)\] for bioenergy production. Algae possess high photosynthetic efficiency and rapid growth rates, allowing for more effective carbon capture \[[41](https://www.mdpi.com/2673-5628/4/4/24#B41-gases-04-00024)\]. In addition to CO2, algae can absorb sulfur dioxide and nitrogen oxides, further contributing to emission reductions \[[42](https://www.mdpi.com/2673-5628/4/4/24#B42-gases-04-00024),[43](https://www.mdpi.com/2673-5628/4/4/24#B43-gases-04-00024)\]. Algae can also thrive in environments that are unsuitable for traditional agriculture, such as polluted water, minimizing competition for arable land and freshwater \[[44](https://www.mdpi.com/2673-5628/4/4/24#B44-gases-04-00024),[45](https://www.mdpi.com/2673-5628/4/4/24#B45-gases-04-00024),[46](https://www.mdpi.com/2673-5628/4/4/24#B46-gases-04-00024)\]. Moreover, algae-based technologies feature lower CO2 storage and operational costs, enhancing their feasibility for large-scale deployment. Abstract
--------

This short paper suggests a review of the latest developments and current challenges associated with [carbon dioxide](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/carbon-dioxide "Learn more about carbon dioxide from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") capture, utilization and storage. Recent research has been conducted to reduce energy consumption, costs, and improve efficiency. In [carbon dioxide](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/chemical-engineering/carbon-dioxide "Learn more about carbon dioxide from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") capture, catalysts have been added to solvents while new membranes and [sorbent](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/sorbent "Learn more about sorbent from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") materials have been investigated. In mineral [carbon dioxide storage](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/carbon-dioxide-storage "Learn more about carbon dioxide storage from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), studies have been carried out to improve reaction rates. Regarding the utilization path, attention has been focused on the development of sustainable chemicals (mainly based on electrochemical conversion), biochemical routes and [power generation](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/power-generation "Learn more about power generation from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"). Considering the respective challenges, future efforts should be focused toward the optimization of these systems at all levels, in addition to a public acceptance and new policies and regulations for their spread.

Introduction
------------

Today, the impacts of [climate change](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/climate-change "Learn more about climate change from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") and global warming are being experienced everywhere and these are the most important concerns recognized by world leaders and experts in the 24th annual UN Climate Change Conference that took place in Poland in 2018 [[1](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2452223624000166#bib1)].

It has been demonstrated that these phenomena are caused primarily by the un-controlled emissions of [carbon dioxide](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/carbon-dioxide "Learn more about carbon dioxide from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") (CO 2) from the industrial sector and distributed sources, contributing to about 71.6% of the overall amount of greenhouse gases. CO 2, in fact, absorbing and trapping heat, causes the Earth's temperature to rise. The only solution to this environmental problem is the reduction of both CO 2 emissions and concentration in the atmosphere. Urgent actions are needed: it has been estimated, in fact, that without an effective intervention, net CO 2 emissions could reach a value of 48–55 Gton/year by 2050, due primarily to the increase of future energy demands [[2](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2452223624000166#bib2)]. Consequently, the atmospheric CO 2 concentration is expected to increase and, in the absence of mitigation, its concentration is projected to double by 2100 compared to the current level of 424 ppm, so that the global mean temperature could increase by up to 6.1°C compared to the 1990 level, a value much higher than the target (1.5°C) established in the COP21 [[3](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2452223624000166#bib3),[4](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2452223624000166#bib4)].

The scientific community agrees that solutions aiming at CO 2 mitigation and removal from the atmosphere are required worldwide to change the direction: a 45% and 100% reduction in anthropogenic CO 2 emissions by 2030 and 2050 has been respectively proposed [[5](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2452223624000166#bib5)].

To achieve these objectives at a global scale and establish a low-carbon economy, technologies for CO 2 capture from a [point source](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/source-point "Learn more about point source from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") or the atmosphere, storage and utilization have been deeply analyzed in the literature and experimented by the most important companies [[6](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2452223624000166#bib6), [7∗∗](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2452223624000166#bib7), [8](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2452223624000166#bib8)]. There are different reviews in the literature about CO 2 storage, utilization and capture but a comprehensive work about recent developments (last two years), challenges and future research is missing and for this reason the proposed work wants to fill this gap.

Recent advancements in carbon capture, utilization, and storage
---------------------------------------------------------------

### CO 2 capture

Chemical and physical absorption, adsorption, [membrane separation](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/membranes-separation "Learn more about membrane separation from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), cryogenic processing and chemical looping combustion are the principal technologies to extract CO 2 from [flue gases](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/flue-gas "Learn more about flue gases from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), with characteristics shown in [Table 1](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2452223624000166#tbl1) [[9](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2452223624000166#bib9),[10](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2452223624000166#bib10)]. However, due to their important role, a continuous research is carried out, so that hybrid technologies, such as a combination of the above, are rapidly evolving as alternative options to reduce costs and energy requirements [[11](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2452223624000166#bib11)]. Other advances for CO 2 capture from exhaust streams include [ionic liquids](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/ionic-liquid "Learn more about ionic liquids from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), [nanofluids](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/nanofluid "Learn more about nanofluids from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), [microencapsulation](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/chemical-engineering/microencapsulation "Learn more about microencapsulation from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), phenoxide salts, enzymes, membranes and [sorbents](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/sorbent "Learn more about sorbents from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") [[12](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2452223624000166#bib12),[13](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2452223624000166#bib13)].

| Physical absorption | Absorption due to the solubility of CO 2 in the solvent governed by Henry's law; the solubility changes with pressure or temperature. | * a)90% of CO 2 separation efficiency * b)Low energy consumption for sorbent regeneration * c)Temperature required for the process is lower than that for chemical methods * d)Low corrosivity and toxicity * e)More economical at a higher CO 2 partial pressure |
| Chemical absorption | CO 2 chemically reacts with the solvent to form unstable salts; upon heating, CO 2 is released. | * a)Most mature technology * b)Simplicity and the possibility of using it with a low CO 2 partial pressure * c)Suitable for retrofit * d)High absorption capacities * e)Product purity >99% vol |
| Adsorption | CO 2 separation in mixed-gas streams by solid-material adsorption; changes of temperature and pressure to achieve CO 2 desorption. | * a)85% of CO 2 separation efficiency * b)Ease of use and maintainability of the installation * c)Since adsorbents can be reused, low waste generation * d)Reversible process (physical adsorption) * e)Large selection of materials with high CO 2 uptake * f)Low energy requirement to regenerate an adsorbent material * g)Wide operability range * h)Possibility to use waste biomass or industrial resides as raw materials for the adsorbent synthesis |
| Membrane | CO 2 from a main stream is separated by using a membrane which acts like filter having a selective permeability. | * a)80% of CO 2 separation efficiency * b)Uncomplicated process * c)No regeneration energy is required * d)Simple modular system * e)No waste streams * f)Higher separation energy efficiency compared to that of absorption and adsorption * g)No additional chemicals in the separation process |
| Cryogenic distillation | Consecutive refrigeration at different condensation temperatures with the condensation of gas mixture to separate CO 2 | * a)High CO 2 purity * b)High separation efficiency (up to 99.9% vol. CO 2) * c)Production of ready to transport, pure liquid CO 2 * d)No need of chemical reagents * e)Suitable for high pressure gas stream with high concentration (>50%vol.) * f)Easy scaled-up to industrial application |
| Calcium looping | CO 2 capture occurs through reversible reactions between CaO and CO 2 | * a)Cheap raw materials for sorbent synthesis * b)Optimal method for retrofit of pulverized coal-fired power plants and sorption-enhanced hydrogen production process |
| Chemical looping combustion | The combustion is divided into intermediate oxidation and reduction processes that are carried out independently, with a solid oxygen carrier moving between the two separated units. The oxygen carrier is first oxidized with air forming an oxide. This oxide is then reduced using a hydrocarbon as a reducer in a second reaction, where CO 2 and H 2 O are released. | * a)Simplicity and the possibility of using it with a low CO 2 partial pressure * b)The exhaust gas from the air reactor is mainly N 2 * c)The exhaust gas stream from the fuel reactor is composed of CO 2 and H 2 O (CO 2 can be easily separated by a condenser) * d)Avoids huge energy penalty and thus less operational costs |

Through the use of ionic liquids, it is expected to improve the capture efficiency and reduce the energy required for solvent regeneration [[14](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2452223624000166#bib14)]. These particular solvents, in fact, are less susceptible to degradation and impurities. On the other hand, the addition of [nanoparticles](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/chemical-engineering/nanoparticle "Learn more about nanoparticles from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") (e.g. SiO 2 or carbon nanotubes) to a solvent, microencapsulation, and phenoxide salts have been suggested as strategies to improve the [absorption performances](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/absorption-performance "Learn more about absorption performances from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") in terms of stability, energy and capture efficiency, and even costs [[13](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2452223624000166#bib13),[15](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2452223624000166#bib15)]. An additional and interesting research area is the use of new type of [carbonic anhydrase](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/carbonic-anhydrase "Learn more about carbonic anhydrase from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") enzymes as catalysts (free or immobilized on different supports) dispersed in the [liquid phase](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/liquid-phase "Learn more about liquid phase from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") which may have energetic and economic advantages compared to the traditional monoethanolamine based solvent and [membrane contactors](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/membrane-contactors "Learn more about membrane contactors from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") [[16](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2452223624000166#bib16)].

Several studies have been conducted to find an [advanced material](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/advanced-material "Learn more about advanced material from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") for membranes (e.g. polymer, ceramic, composite metals, [Metal Organic Frameworks](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/metal-organic-framework "Learn more about Metal Organic Frameworks from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") (MOFs), [zeolites](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/zeolite "Learn more about zeolites from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), mixed matrix, [graphene oxide](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/graphene-oxide "Learn more about graphene oxide from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), polyimide) characterized by a good selectivity toward CO 2, low energy consumption, reduced space requirements [[7](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2452223624000166#bib7),[12](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2452223624000166#bib12),[17](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2452223624000166#bib17)]. In addition, the doping of membranes with ionic liquids and [nanoparticles](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/nanoparticle "Learn more about nanoparticles from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") is under investigation [[18](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2452223624000166#bib18)].

New physical sorbents have been investigated and considered highly promising such as [carbon nanotubes](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/chemical-engineering/carbon-nanotube "Learn more about carbon nanotubes from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") or multi-wall carbon nanotubes, [carbon dots](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/carbon-dot "Learn more about carbon dots from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), graphite [oxide](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/oxide-compound "Learn more about oxide from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), MOFs, [porous carbons](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/porous-carbon "Learn more about porous carbons from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), porous [organic polymers](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/organic-polymer "Learn more about organic polymers from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), and [covalent organic frameworks](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/covalent-organic-framework "Learn more about covalent organic frameworks from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") [[5](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2452223624000166#bib5),[19](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2452223624000166#bib19)]. In addition to their synthesis, the advances have been directed to implement these discoveries aiming to improve capture capacity and long-term stability/durability [[17](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2452223624000166#bib17)]. In recent studies, process configurations have been also suggested: CO 2 capture via [CaO](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/chemical-engineering/lime "Learn more about CaO from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") sorbent has been integrated into utilization to produce [syngas](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/syngas "Learn more about syngas from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") [[20](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2452223624000166#bib20)] and recycled methane [[21](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2452223624000166#bib21)].

Regarding CO 2 capture from the air, the most investigated and mature technologies are the absorption or adsorption that are adopted by the main companies as shown in [Table 2](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2452223624000166#tbl2) [[22](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2452223624000166#bib22)]. Studies are currently also carried out for membrane and electrochemical based technologies [[12](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2452223624000166#bib12),[17](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2452223624000166#bib17)]. Regarding adsorption, materials with good CO 2[adsorption capacities](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/adsorption-capacity "Learn more about adsorption capacities from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") and low regeneration energy have been investigated: MOFs [[23](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2452223624000166#bib23)], amine functionalized sorbents [[24](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2452223624000166#bib24)], zeolites [[25](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2452223624000166#bib25)], carbon-based materials [[26](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2452223624000166#bib26)], porous [organic polymers](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/chemical-engineering/organic-polymer "Learn more about organic polymers from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") [[27](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2452223624000166#bib27)], porous [covalent organic frameworks](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/covalent-organic-framework "Learn more about covalent organic frameworks from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") [[28](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2452223624000166#bib28)], [silica](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/silicon-dioxide "Learn more about silica from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") and silica-amine based sorbents, hybrid ultra [microporous materials](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/microporous-material "Learn more about microporous materials from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), biochar, and metal oxide-based sorbents [[17](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2452223624000166#bib17)]. Innovative membrane materials to improve the selectivity are currently under research: [mixed matrix membranes](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/mixed-matrix-membrane "Learn more about mixed matrix membranes from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") [[29](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2452223624000166#bib29)], supported ionic [liquid membranes](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/liquid-membrane "Learn more about liquid membranes from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") [[30](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2452223624000166#bib30)], MOF membranes [[17](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2452223624000166#bib17)]. Current findings of synthetic strategies for several functional and redox-active organic sorbents with good CO 2 selectivity, solvent stability, electrode design, and defined physical properties are under investigation for the electrochemical capture route [[31](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2452223624000166#bib31)]. For the [absorption system](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/absorption-system "Learn more about absorption system from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), amino acid and peptide salt solutions have been proposed due to a good resistance toward[thermal degradation](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/thermal-degradation "Learn more about thermal degradation from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") and [oxidation](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/oxidation-reaction "Learn more about oxidation from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), low [corrosivity](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/corrosivity "Learn more about corrosivity from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") and toxicity, fast CO 2 reaction rates [[17](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2452223624000166#bib17)].

### CO 2 storage

CO 2 storage technologies commonly include geological, [mineral carbonation](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/mineral-carbonation "Learn more about mineral carbonation from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), terrestrial and ocean storage options as analyzed in [Table 3](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2452223624000166#tbl3). Among them, electrochemical, microbial, and supercritical CO 2[mineralization](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/mineralisation "Learn more about mineralization from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") storages are the latest advancements in this subarea with the aim to accelerate reaction rates and reduce energy consumption [[12](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2452223624000166#bib12)]. Ongoing research in [biochar production](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/biochar-production "Learn more about biochar production from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") and soil amendments is under development to increase the efficiency of terrestrial storage potentially able to permanently remove from the air 2.879 ton CO 2eq per ton of dry biochar [[12](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2452223624000166#bib12),[32](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2452223624000166#bib32)]. [Ocean fertilization](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/ocean-fertilisation "Learn more about Ocean fertilization from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), artificial upwelling, ocean alkalinity enhancement, and marine permaculture are currently under investigation to increase efficiency and sustainability in the ocean storage technique [[12](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2452223624000166#bib12)].

enriched_points.expanded_content: Different industrial processes emit CO₂ at varying concentrations, impacting algae's ability to efficiently capture carbon. This variability complicates the design of effective algal systems.
Scaling up algal carbon capture operations from pilot to industrial levels poses significant challenges, including cost, infrastructure, and maintaining optimal growth conditions for algae.
Existing carbon capture technologies face limitations in efficiency and integration with algal systems, hindering advancements in algal-based carbon capture and utilization.
